source.definition: to cause someone to become weary or exhausted, either physically or mentally

explanation.sourceText: The English verb "tire" originates from Old French "tirer" meaning 'to pull, drag', which evolved in Middle English to mean 'to grow weary or exhausted'.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „tire” provine din franceza veche „tirer”, care înseamnă „a trage, a smulge”, și a evoluat în engleza mijlocie cu sensul de „a deveni obosit sau epuizat”.

source.additional: Regular verb. Used to describe becoming physically or mentally fatigued.

target.definition: a face pe cineva să fie obosit sau epuizat

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb "a obosi" comes from Slavic, probably from Bulgarian "obesja" or Serbian "obesiti", meaning "to become tired".
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a obosi” provine din slavă, probabil din bulgară „obesja” sau sârbă „obesiti”, cu sensul de „a deveni obosit”.

target.additional: Regular verb of the 1st conjugation (-i). Commonly used to express the state of tiredness.

examples.sourceText: I tire after a long day.
examples.targetText: Eu obosesc după o zi lungă.

examples.sourceText: I did not tire myself at all.
I will tire if I don't sleep.

learningTips.text: The Romanian verb "a obosi" sounds like "oboseală" (fatigue) – connect both words in your mind with the feeling of tiredness.
Imagine your body as a battery running low – this helps you remember "a obosi" means 'to get tired'.
Think of the English word 'obese' to recall 'a obosi' because when you're tired, you might feel heavy or sluggish.
Link the sound 'obo' in "a obosi" to 'oboe' – after playing a long oboe piece, you might feel tired!
